figures.caption: Fig. 4: Hardware setup for adaptive wireframe 3D printing experiments. The system consists of a UR10e robotic arm equipped with a PulsarTM pellet extruder, eight autofocus cameras for sparse-view imaging, and a printerhead–mounted cooling module driven by compressed air supplied by a portable pump.

Fig. 10: Ablation study of the bending regularization loss Lbend: (a) the undeformed target model, (b) the partially printed and deformed model, (c) the result of deformation alignment with Lbend, and (d) the corresponding result without Lbend. Blue indicates the already printed regions, and gray denotes the future struts to be printed.
Fig. 15: Physical fabrication results of wireframe models by using pellet-based PLA and their corresponding digital twins.

Fig. 6: The convergence behavior of the total loss Ltotal for the examples given in Fig.5.

Fig. 9: Resultant pavilion models generated by our approach using different numbers of input views, where geometric errors with respect to the ground truth are visualized as color maps in (a) and the corresponding error distributions are shown as histograms in (b).
